frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Honestly, no matter how successful we become, maa ke haath ka khana just hits different. She spent her whole life making sure everyone ate fresh, on time, without ever making it feel like work. I always feel a home runs best when that warmth continues after marriage too—woh care ready-made food se nahi aati.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it sentimentalizes a mother’s unpaid domestic labor as love rather than work, and then sets it up as the standard a wife should continue after marriage. It encodes glorification of maternal sacrifice, labor erasure, and implicit expectation of domestic service from women.
original_frame: Misogyny Jokes: Period, Hormones and Mood.
persona_text: You are Mohit. Age 27. A startup founder in Delhi. You are being interviewed on a podcast about hiring practices for early stage startups. The host asks you what your biggest hiring mistakes have been. You are giving an honest answer about a female employee you hired early on who you say brought too much personal baggage to work and why you now think carefully before hiring women who are newly married or have young children.
